1.synthesis: God's answer to Moses's lament is the greatest covenant-disclosure in the Exodus narrative so far. It begins with confidence: 'Now you shall see what I will do to Pharaoh; for with a strong hand he will send them out, and with a strong hand he will drive them out of his land' (v.1). Then the name-declaration: 'I am YHWH' (v.2). The patriarchs knew God as El Shaddai (God Almighty) but 'by my name YHWH I did not make myself known to them' (v.3). The distinction is not of name but of covenant experience: the patriarchs received the promises; Israel will see them fulfilled. The seven-fold promise that follows (vv.6–8) is the fullest covenant statement in the OT to this point: I will bring out … I will deliver … I will redeem … I will take you … I will be your God … you will know that I am YHWH your God … I will bring you into the land. But when Moses repeats this to Israel, 'they did not listen to Moses, because of their broken spirit and harsh slavery' (v.9).
1.historical_context: The distinction between El Shaddai (the patriarchal name) and YHWH (the Exodus name) in v.3 has been central to source-critical debate. The resolution most consistent with the text is that the name YHWH was not wholly unknown to the patriarchs (they used it in Genesis) but its full covenant meaning — YHWH as the one who delivers, redeems, and makes a people for himself — was not yet enacted. The patriarchs received promises; Exodus receives fulfilment. The seven-fold promise structure of vv.6–8 follows ANE treaty form: the suzerain (YHWH) recites his acts, states his obligations, and declares the covenant relationship. 'I will take you to be my people, and I will be your God' (v.7) is the Exodus covenant formula that will reappear throughout the OT as the standard expression of covenant relationship.
1.literary_structure: The seven-fold promise (vv.6–8: bring out, deliver, redeem, take, be your God, bring into the land) is the Exodus's covenant manifesto. Each 'I will' is a divine commitment that Exodus-Numbers will unfold: the bringing out (Passover), the delivering (Red Sea), the redeeming (Sinai covenant), the taking as his people (the forty years), the being their God (the tabernacle), the bringing into the land (Joshua). The tragedy of v.9 — 'they did not listen … because of their broken spirit' — prepares for the recurring pattern of Exodus: divine promise, human inability to receive it, divine persistence. The promise is not withdrawn because it is not heard; YHWH sends Moses to Pharaoh again regardless.
1.theological_application: Gill on 'by my name YHWH I was not known to them': 'Not that the name Jehovah was unknown to the patriarchs … but the full and clear meaning of it, its virtue and efficacy, as displayed in the deliverance of his people from their bondage and slavery, they had no knowledge of, or experience in.' Matthew Henry: 'The name of God here used is Jehovah, the name that denotes his self-existence, his eternity, and his immutability — the name by which he will be known for his faithfulness in performing what was promised to the fathers.' The covenant formula — 'I will be your God, and you shall be my people' — is the seed of all subsequent covenant theology, expanded in the Davidic covenant and fulfilled in the new covenant: 'I will be their God and they shall be my people' (Jeremiah 31:33; Revelation 21:3).
1.christology: The seven-fold 'I will' of vv.6–8 is the OT's most concentrated expression of divine unilateral redemption — salvation entirely by divine act, not human merit. Paul's theology of grace builds on this pattern: 'by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God' (Ephesians 2:8). The Exodus redemption — I will redeem you with an outstretched arm (v.6) — is applied to the cross in 1 Peter 1:18–19: 'you were ransomed … not with perishable things such as silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ, like that of a lamb without blemish.' The 'stretched out arm' of YHWH at the Red Sea and the outstretched arms of Christ on the cross are the two great enactments of the divine redeeming will.

10.synthesis: YHWH commands Moses and Aaron again: go to Pharaoh, tell him to release Israel from Egypt (vv.10–11). Moses responds with the same protest he made at the bush: 'Behold, the people of Israel have not listened to me. How then shall Pharaoh listen to me, for I am of uncircumcised lips?' (v.12). The metaphor 'uncircumcised lips' is the inverse of the covenant sign: just as an uncircumcised man stands outside the covenant community, Moses feels his speech is unreceived, unfit, unable to accomplish its purpose. YHWH renews the commission regardless, this time charging both Moses and Aaron together: 'to bring the people of Israel out of the land of Egypt' (v.13). The divine persistence against prophetic reluctance is itself theologically significant: God's mission does not depend on the instrument's confidence in itself.
10.historical_context: The phrase 'uncircumcised lips' (ʿăral śĕpātayim, literally 'foreskinned of lips') appears in Exodus only (here and in 6:30), though 'uncircumcised heart' appears in Leviticus and Deuteronomy. The metaphor draws on the covenant sign of circumcision as a marker of covenant fitness: Moses experiences his speech as uncircumcised — unapproved, unready, unfit for the divine purpose. The repetition of Moses's objection after ch.4's lengthy commissioning shows that self-distrust is not cured by a single divine encounter. The charge to 'Moses and Aaron' together (v.13) is a narrative development: the earlier charge was primarily to Moses, with Aaron as auxiliary; now they are co-commissioned, preparing for the shared role they will play in the plague narrative.
10.literary_structure: This brief unit (four verses) serves as a transition between the great covenant promise of vv.1–9 and the genealogical interruption of vv.14–27. It also repeats and anchors the commissioning: after the genealogy that establishes who Moses and Aaron are, the narrative returns to this exact point (v.28–30 resumes from v.12–13). The repetition creates a literary bracket around the genealogy — the genealogy is embedded in the recommissioning narrative. Moses's 'uncircumcised lips' complaint mirrors his 'not eloquent' complaint in 4:10: despite all the divine promises, the prophet's self-estimate remains low. The mission goes forward not on Moses's confidence but on divine insistence.
10.theological_application: Gill on 'uncircumcised lips': 'by which he means that he was not fit and proper to be employed in such a service, being no orator, but a man of stammering and hesitating speech; and though the Lord had promised to be with his mouth, and teach him what to say, he had not yet had experience of it.' Matthew Henry: 'Moses still insists on his own unfitness. God's grace and God's word are not enough to silence his diffidence. Yet God does not rebuke him for it, but renews the commission.' The divine persistence — commanding again despite Moses's repeated objection — models the patience of God with his reluctant servants. The history of Moses, Jonah, Jeremiah, and the apostles demonstrates that God regularly commissions those who resist the commission and works through their weakness rather than waiting for their confidence.
10.christology: 'Uncircumcised lips' is the OT figure for speech that is unfit for covenant purpose. In the NT, the outpouring of the Spirit at Pentecost fulfills the promise of 'circumcised' lips: the same disciples who fled at the crucifixion speak with boldness (Acts 2:4, 4:31) — not because of their own eloquence but because the Spirit gives the utterance. Paul's 'I will boast all the more gladly of my weaknesses, so that the power of Christ may rest upon me' (2 Corinthians 12:9) is the NT resolution of the uncircumcised-lips problem: the weakness of the instrument is the occasion for the power of the Spirit. Moses's lips become the medium for the most important confrontation in Israel's national history — not because Moses overcomes his weakness but because God speaks through it.

14.synthesis: The narrative pauses for a genealogical record that traces the line through Reuben (Jacob's firstborn, vv.14–15), Simeon (v.15), and Levi (vv.16–25) to establish Moses and Aaron's place in the tribal structure. The Levitical genealogy is detailed: Levi's sons, Gershon, Kohath, Merari; Kohath's son Amram and his wife Jochebed; Amram and Jochebed's children Aaron, Moses, and Miriam (vv.16–20). Aaron's sons are listed with special attention: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar — the four Aaronic priests (vv.23–25). Eleazar's line through Phinehas receives particular mention (v.25). The unit ends with the formal identification: 'These are the Aaron and Moses to whom the LORD said, "Bring out the people of Israel from the land of Egypt"' (vv.26–27). The genealogy locates the Exodus leaders within covenant history before recommissioning them.
14.historical_context: The genealogy's placement here — after the burning bush, after the signs, after the first failed confrontation with Pharaoh — is the biblical convention of introducing key figures with genealogical credentials before their major acts. Reuben and Simeon are mentioned but receive no genealogical elaboration, serving only to establish the ordinal position before reaching Levi. The Levi genealogy is detailed because the priestly-levitical function is the goal: by the end of Exodus, the Levites will be consecrated for tabernacle service, Aaron for the high priesthood, and Aaron's sons for the ordinary priesthood. The lifespan of Levi (137 years), Kohath (133), and Amram (137) in vv.16–20 are the last patriarchal-style lifespans in the OT — the decreasing numbers of the post-Flood era reaching their floor.
14.literary_structure: The genealogy serves as a formal identification and credentialing of Moses and Aaron before the plague narrative begins. The repetition in v.26–27 — 'these are the Aaron and Moses' and 'these are the Moses and Aaron' (reversing the order) — formally closes the genealogy and re-identifies the two men with the divine commission. The bracket-structure (commissioning, vv.10–13; genealogy, vv.14–27; recommissioning, vv.28–30; ch.7 begins the plagues) is the narrator's way of pausing before the extended plague narrative to ground it in covenant history: the Exodus is not a random event but the fulfillment of the promises made to Abraham's children's children's children.
14.theological_application: Gill on the Levitical genealogy: 'this pedigree is given, partly to show the lineal descent of Moses and Aaron … as well as to prevent any charge against them, of being persons of ignoble birth, and not of the seed of Abraham, or of one of the tribes of Israel.' Matthew Henry: 'The sacred history takes occasion to insert the pedigree of Moses and Aaron, that we might know who those were that were employed in this great work, and the honour of their extraction.' The genealogy establishes Moses and Aaron as legitimate members of Israel — not outsiders claiming authority but Levites with specific tribal credentials. The attention given to Aaron's sons (Eleazar, Phinehas) anticipates the Aaronic priestly succession that will continue throughout the OT period.
14.christology: The Levitical genealogy that culminates in Aaron as high priest anticipates the priesthood that Hebrews 7 will interpret as a type ultimately fulfilled in Christ. Aaron and his sons are 'types and shadows' (cf. Hebrews 8:5) of the true high priest who offered himself once for all. The Aaronic priest entered the Holy of Holies annually with blood not his own; Christ entered 'the greater and more perfect tent … through his own blood, securing an eternal redemption' (Hebrews 9:11–12). Moses, the mediator who brought the law; Aaron, the high priest who brought sacrifice — both point to the one who is 'the mediator of a better covenant' (Hebrews 8:6) and the great high priest who has passed through the heavens (Hebrews 4:14).

28.synthesis: The narrative resumes where it paused for the genealogy: 'On the day when YHWH spoke to Moses in the land of Egypt, YHWH said to Moses, "I am YHWH; tell Pharaoh king of Egypt all that I say to you"' (vv.28–29). Moses's response repeats v.12 almost verbatim: 'Behold, I am of uncircumcised lips. How will Pharaoh listen to me?' (v.30). The brevity of the unit — three verses — is not redundancy but the narrator's deliberate resumption: the genealogy has established who these men are; the divine commissioning holds regardless of prophetic self-doubt; the plagues are about to begin. The repetition of 'I am YHWH' as both the content of Moses's message and the ground of his authority frames the entire plague sequence: every confrontation with Pharaoh is a demonstration of who YHWH is.
28.historical_context: The resumption formula 'on the day when YHWH spoke to Moses in the land of Egypt' (v.28) is a narrative device linking what follows directly to the Sinai commission — the entire Exodus from burning bush through plagues through Passover is a single continuous divine act. The self-declaration 'I am YHWH' in v.29 frames the plague narrative's theology: each plague will conclude with 'by this you shall know that I am YHWH' or 'that there is none like me in all the earth' (e.g., 7:17; 8:22; 9:14). The plagues are not primarily punitive but revelatory — they are YHWH's answer to Pharaoh's 'I do not know YHWH.' Moses's repeated 'uncircumcised lips' objection at this structural transition point signals that the divine initiative always precedes and overcomes prophetic reluctance.
28.literary_structure: The three-verse unit functions as a hinge between the genealogical interruption and the plague narrative that begins in ch.7. Its structural role is to re-anchor the narrative in the divine commission — after fourteen verses of genealogy, the reader is returned to Moses and his persistent reluctance, and the divine command that transcends it. The parallel with the ch.3–4 commissioning is exact: at the bush, Moses objected repeatedly; God answered; Moses still objected. Here again, Moses objects; God reiterates the command. The plague narrative that follows is thus launched from a position of human inadequacy and divine insistence — the Exodus proceeds entirely on the strength of 'I am YHWH,' not 'Moses is eloquent.'
28.theological_application: Gill on the resumption: 'The Lord said to him as before, he repeated the same commission he had before given him, to engage him to proceed in the work he had called him to, notwithstanding his objections and expostulations.' Matthew Henry: 'God here silences the remaining objections of Moses by again giving him authority … The Lord here calls himself "YHWH" to encourage Moses: the God who IS, who cannot fail, whose word cannot return void.' The repetition of the commission despite repeated objection is a pastoral word for every reluctant servant: God does not retract his call when his servant doubts their own adequacy. The mission stands on divine authority, not human confidence.
28.christology: 'I am YHWH; tell Pharaoh king of Egypt all that I say to you' (v.29) — the Exodus message is delivered by the mouth of an 'uncircumcised-lipped' prophet, not by a compelling orator. The power is not in the speaker but in the name. In Christ, this reaches its ultimate resolution: the eternal Word himself becomes flesh and speaks — no uncircumcised lips, no need for an Aaron, no reluctance. John 12:50 shows Christ as the perfect prophetic speaker: 'I know that his commandment is eternal life. What I say, therefore, I say as the Father has told me.' Moses's difficulty in speaking precisely what God commanded finds its resolution in the Son who speaks only what he hears from the Father and speaks it without defect.
